narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 50% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was the key reason your application was rejected—they use this prediction to gauge future success. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an LSAT score of 30, an undergraduate GPA of 2.8, coming from a lower-middle income family, identifying as a Native American woman planning to study full-time. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who average around the middle deciles for school grades, LSAT scores near 37, GPAs over 3.2, and mostly from higher income brackets with White backgrounds—your academic metrics stand out as notably lower, while your demographic traits are less common. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your third-year school decile ranking, which had the biggest influence and pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure because it was so much lower than average. Next in influence was your LSAT score, also pulling firmly toward failure due to being well below the typical range. Your first-year decile followed closely, again contributing to the failure prediction with its low standing relative to peers. Your racial background as Native American had a moderate influence in the same direction, as it's underrepresented and associated with different outcomes in the data. Family income level and undergraduate GPA each played supporting roles toward the failure prediction, given their below-average positions. Whether you'll study full-time and your gender had the smallest impacts, both nudging slightly toward failure but not driving the outcome. Overall, these factors combined to tip the model to a borderline failure risk it deemed too high for admission. To improve your chances next time, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT with targeted prep to boost your score closer to average, build stronger academic habits through advanced coursework or tutoring to mimic higher decile performance, and invest in bar exam-specific study strategies early, like practice tests and weak-area drills. Your full-time commitment is a plus aligned with most successful students, so lean into that dedication. With these steps, you could shift future predictions in your favor and show admissions committees real growth. Keep pushing—you have the drive to turn this around.
